words.definition_en: A large bird that no longer exists.
Huge extinct bird from Madagascar island.
Striped animal like zebra, now gone.
Large seal that hunts in the ocean.
Giant hairy elephant from ice age.
When all animals of a kind die.
To stay alive and keep living.
To use something in a bad way.
In danger of dying out completely.
Place where animals live and find food.
A group of animals that are alike.
Safe place where animals are protected.

grammar.point: Present Perfect and Present Perfect Continuous
examples: I have read books about dodos.
I have been reading this book for an hour.
They've been playing basketball since three o'clock.
I've been learning English for four years.
rules: Use the present perfect to say what you've done in your life up to now.
Use the present perfect continuous to say that you started doing something in the past and you're still doing it now.
The present perfect continuous uses: have/has + been + verb-ing.

words.definition_en: Many different kinds or types of things.
To recognize who or what something is.
Someone who follows and watches animals.
To keep someone or something safe from harm.
A person who studies plants and animals in nature.
Illegally hunting or catching animals without permission.
Broken or harmed so it does not work right.
Protecting and caring for nature and wildlife.
Keeping something safe and in good condition.
To make someone want to do or create something.
All the animals of one kind living in one place.
A protected area where animals can live safely.

examples: Tigers go to the river every day.
Tigers are going to the river to drink.
Tigers went to the river yesterday.
People have cleared forests to grow crops.
The animals' habitat has suffered.
Tigers have always lived in thick jungles.
rules: Simple Present: use for habits and facts.
Present Continuous: use for actions happening now.
Simple Past: use for completed past actions.
Present Perfect: use for things that happened in the past but have an effect now, or started in the past and continue now.

topic: The Cavity and Have You Any Idea?

words.definition_en: Flat front teeth for cutting food.
Sharp pointed teeth for tearing.
Big flat back teeth for grinding.
Animal that eats only meat.
Animal that eats only plants.
Animal that eats meat and plants.
To cut something into thin pieces.
To hold something tightly in hand.
To squeeze something until it breaks.
Hole in tooth from tooth decay.
Pink soft tissue around your teeth.
Bones in your face that hold teeth.

examples: I could read by the time I was four.
I couldn't chew after I got a cavity.
Next year, I will be able to ride my bike to school.
I won't be able to drive a car.
rules: Use could and couldn't to say what you did or didn't know how to do in the past.
The future of can/can't is will/won't be able to.
Use will be able to for future ability.

words.definition_en: Sharp tooth used to bite or inject venom.
Long pointed tooth sticking out of mouth.
Poison that some animals use to hurt others.
A safe place to stay and be protected.
Wall built across water to hold it back.
Animal hunted and eaten by another animal.
To bite and chew something hard repeatedly.
To push something sharp through something.
To push away or defend against something.
To make something weaker through time.
To drink liquid with tongue like a cat.
To make something new that did not exist.

examples: It must be a prosimian. It has comb-like lower teeth.
It has to be a spider. It has eight legs.
That can't be a spider! It only has four legs.
It might be a spider, but I'm not sure.
rules: Use must or has to to say you're sure that something is true.
Use can't to say you're sure that something isn't true.
Use might if you're not sure that something is true.

words.definition_en: Ways to move people and things from place to place.
Move something from one place to another.
Pull or carry something heavy with effort.
Rod that wheels spin around on vehicles.
Turn around and around in circles.
Thin rods connecting wheel center to rim.
Outer edge of a wheel or circular object.
Grip between tires and ground for moving.
Wheel with teeth that turns other wheels.
Crush something into powder with pressure.
Tools and machines humans invent and use.
Long thin stick or bar made of metal.

grammar.point: Future with Will and Going to
examples: The sun will rise at 6:05 tomorrow.
Wheels will always be a central part of life.
I'm going to visit my uncle next week.
Look at those big, black clouds. It's going to rain.
rules: Use will to talk about facts in the future or to make predictions about things you believe will be true.
Use going to to talk about future plans or to make predictions about things you see.
Will is used for future facts and predictions based on belief; going to is used for future plans and predictions based on what you see.

words.definition_en: Doing what you are told to do.
Lucky, having good things happen.
Boring and takes a long time.
Making you feel happy and interested.
Breaks easily, needs careful handling.
Wanting something and not giving up.
Brave enough to do something risky.
Not smart, making silly mistakes.
Very exciting and making your heart race.
Very brave when facing something scary.
Might result in something bad happening.
Very interested and wanting to know more.

examples: We are meeting our friends at 2:00 on Friday.
The moon will rise at ten o'clock tonight.
Things will never be the same again.
I'm going to take a music class next year.
Look at those clouds. We're going to get wet.
rules: You can use the present continuous to talk about future arrangements, especially when you mention a specific time or place.
Use will for future facts and predictions about things you believe will be true.
Use going to for future plans and predictions about things you see.

words.definition_en: Run or move very fast to win.
Move slowly without using much power.
Move smoothly and quietly without effort.
Walk through water slowly and carefully.
Move a boat using an oar or paddle.
Jump head first into water below.
Water flying up and making wet spots.
Push or move something forward fast.
Stay on top of water without sinking.
Run as fast as you can for short time.
Run at a slow and steady speed.
Walk slowly and heavily with effort.

examples: "It's a beautiful day," said Magdi. → Magdi said that it was a beautiful day.
"I'm working," said Tom. → Tom said that he was working.
He said that he was going fishing.
She said that she didn't like cats.
rules: Reported speech tells what someone said by using the words said that.
If the direct speech is in the present tense, the reported speech changes to the past tense.
Add said that and change the verb to past tense when reporting what someone said.

words.definition_en: Willing to talk and share information.
To find where something is.
To work together with other people.
To copy what someone else does.
To act in a certain way.
To show or point something out.
To make someone interested or like it.
To say you will hurt someone.
To protect from harm or attack.
To move closer to someone.
To show your feelings or ideas.
To make or create something new.

grammar.point: Reported Speech with Told or Asked
examples: He told us that Kanzi was learning new things every day.
A classmate asked the scientist what Kanzi was learning.
My teacher told me that Kanzi was learning 450 words.
I asked how many words Kanzi was learning.
rules: Use said that to report what someone said.
You can also use told or asked to report speech.
After told or asked, you need to add an object pronoun or someone's name.
If the direct speech is in the present tense, the reported speech changes to past tense.

bigQuestion: What do different cultures give to the world?

words.definition_en: The way people live and their beliefs.
A group of people with government and rules.
Something helpful you give or do.
Growing and becoming bigger and better.
Something great you finish or accomplish.
A tool or machine that does something.
The steps to do something from start to end.
Something families do the same way always.
A special way people do things normally.
Something good that helps or improves you.
A special event with important actions.
Something made or grown to be sold.

examples: Who saw Finn?
Who did Finn see?
Finn's mom saw Finn.
Finn didn't see anybody.
rules: Ask questions with who, where, when, why, what, which, and how.
Be careful: Who saw Finn? (Who is the subject.) vs. Who did Finn see? (Who is the object.)
When who is the subject, do not use do/does/did in the question.

bigQuestion: What do different cultures give to the world?

words.definition_en: Food that tastes really good and yummy.
Smooth and thick like cream or butter.
Food with hot pepper that burns mouth.
Tastes like nuts or has nut flavor.
Big spoon for serving soup and liquids.
Things that stick to your hands easily.
Makes a loud cracking sound when chewed.
Liquid or flows easily like thin soup.
No bumps and very soft to touch.
Fast spinning sound like a machine makes.
Very loud deep sound that echoes.
Small mark or stain on something.

examples: Is there a chocolate shop near here? Yes, there is.
Do you like my new sneakers? Yes, I do!
Has it stopped raining? No, it hasn't.
Are you 15 years old? No, I'm not!
rules: Short answers include: Yes, I am; No, he didn't; Yes, they can; No, we won't; Yes, there is.
Using a short answer is more polite than answering only Yes or No.
Short answers repeat the auxiliary verb from the question.

words.definition_en: Huge sheet of ice that moves slowly.
Many connected mountains in a line.
The highest point of a mountain.
How high something is above ground.
Low area of land between mountains.
Going up or down at sharp angle.
Surface that goes up or down gradually.
A raised piece of land, smaller mountain.
Flat step cut into a hillside for farming.
Works well and does what it should.
Water moves between earth and sky.
Water that floats in air as gas.

examples: People find corn in the mountains. → Corn is found in the mountains.
Do people grow rice in Canada? → Is rice grown in Canada?
Pizza is eaten in many countries.
Mountains are called the water towers of the world.
rules: We can say the same thing in two different ways by making active or passive sentences.
Using the passive is a good idea if you don't know exactly who or what does something.
Form the passive with: subject + is/are + past participle.

words.definition_en: Going on and on without stopping.
Speak with stops and repeated sounds.
Amazed and impressed by something great.
Having sharp rough edges that stick out.
Having a sharp point at the end.
Very small copy of something bigger.
Serious and strict without being friendly.
Curved shape like a rainbow or bridge.
Speak quietly so others cannot hear well.
Twist and turn your body uncomfortably.
Frown with an angry unhappy expression.
Make something longer or pull it tight.

examples: Someone broke my mom's favorite vase. → My mom's favorite vase was broken.
Someone showed the weather forecasts on TV. → The weather forecasts were shown on TV.
Mount Everest was climbed for the first time in 1953.
Last year, a big party was organized in my town.
rules: You can use the active or the passive to say the same thing in two different ways.
You can also do this in the past tense.
Form the past passive with: subject + was/were + past participle.

topic: The Sound of Money and the Smell of Soup

words.definition_en: Very scary and makes you feel afraid.
Feeling worried or scared about something.
Not polite or mean to other people.
Being kind and respectful to others.
Wild and angry or very strong.
Attacking or acting in a mean way.
Very soft and easily broken or hurt.
Doing something even when you are scared.
Wanting more food or things than needed.
Feeling nervous around other people quietly.
Knowing lots of things and making good choices.
Staying quiet and peaceful without worry.

examples: A terrifying man with huge muscles opened the door.
"Three gold coins!" repeated the man aggressively.
The Hodja was reading a newspaper, as he often did.
The little boy greedily ate all the grapes.
rules: Adjectives tell you more about nouns.
Adverbs tell you more about verbs. An adverb of manner tells you how something happens.
An adverb of frequency tells you how often something happens.

words.definition_en: Place where people keep money safe.
Money used in a country.
Round metal pieces of money.
Trade things without using money.
Make something look exactly same.
Paper money or amount you owe.
Use money to buy things.
Give money for something you want.
Take something to return later.
Trade one thing for another thing.
Need to give back money later.
Give something to someone temporarily.

examples: If you had paper money, you wouldn't need so many coins.
If I had a bike, I would get to school quicker.
If my friend could fly a plane, we would go to the North Pole.
If I won a prize, I would be very happy.
rules: Use the unreal conditional to talk about things that aren't possible now.
Form: If + past tense, subject + would + verb.
The situation is imaginary or unlikely in the present or future.

words.definition_en: Plan how something will look.
New and different way of doing.
Very special and worth noticing.
Wanting to achieve big goals.
Lasting forever and not changing.
Move to a place and stay there.
Come out from somewhere slowly.
Made by people not nature.
Help something become more popular.
Surround or close something completely.
Right and good for something.
Helpful and good for someone.

examples: The sculptures look bumpy and rough.
I felt sick this morning, but I'm OK now.
You look happy!
This lemon smells good, but it tastes bitter.
Your new song sounds fantastic!
rules: Sense verbs (look, feel, sound, smell, taste) are followed by an adjective, not an adverb.
Do not use an adverb after a sense verb.
Examples of sense verbs: look, feel, sound, smell, taste.

words.definition_en: Small house made of wood.
Person who gives helpful suggestions.
Flat area on a ship or boat.
Feeling thankful for something nice.
Very strong storm with lots wind.
Rescue someone from danger or harm.
When friends or couples stop together.
Respond to something that happens.
Bad person who causes trouble.
Raised platform where actors perform.
Person who writes plays and stories.
All the scenery and props onstage.

examples: It's difficult to save the passengers and crew.
It's fun to climb a tree.
It's easy to learn English if you read a lot of books.
It's fun to act in a play!
rules: Use it's + adjective + infinitive as subject to express an opinion.
The infinitive (to + verb) acts as the subject of the sentence.
This structure avoids starting a sentence with a verb-ing subject.

words.definition_en: Large place where sports games happen.
Tall building with bright light for ships.
Very tall narrow building or structure.
Extremely tall building that touches sky.
Big building where goods are stored.
Huge fancy home of a king or queen.
Building where judges decide legal cases.
Place to watch plays and movies.
Building for listening to music performances.
Place where people work at desks.
Building where paintings and art displayed.
Store displaying products for people to see.

grammar.point: A, An, The, and No Article
examples: Before a building can be built, the building needs to be designed.
It's a kangaroo.
It's in the pool.
I play tennis. I saw Jim at lunch. We went to Spain. They like math.
rules: Use a or an the first time you talk about something. Use the when you talk about it again.
Use a or an with things that aren't special (not a specific one).
Use the when the person you're talking to knows what you're talking about.
Don't use an article to talk about sports, meals, most countries, and school subjects.

words.definition_en: Small towers on castle corners.
Buildings where horses are kept.
Building at entrance to protect gate.
Water-filled ditch around castle.
Bridge that can be pulled up.
Large room for meetings or events.
Open area surrounded by buildings.
Thick castle walls for protection.
Speak quietly and unclearly.
Become stiff or hard and rigid.
Not here or cannot be found.
Shake slightly from fear or cold.

grammar.point: Review of Tenses and Future Forms
examples: I needed to find the coin.
They had to solve the mystery.
I like castles with turrets.
We need the other half of the coin.
Are you going to explore Peril Castle?
I will see the castles of Wales someday.
rules: Use different tenses and future forms to describe different times.
Past: use simple past or past forms (needed, had to) for completed past actions.
Present: use simple present (like, need) for habits and facts.
Future: use going to for plans and will for predictions and facts.
